title: Somalia is the dark horse in US defense and economic strategy
gist: Former DoD/NSC officials argue Somalia should be reframed from a pure counterterrorism problem into an overlooked frontier investment and geostrategic asset, given its position astride the Bab al-Mandeb and the Red Sea trade arteries that the Houthi disruption is choking. They urge the US to tie security assistance to economic objectives in Jubaland, Puntland and Somaliland, where terrorist threats, cooperative partners and resource potential converge, and to counter Turkey's integrated security-energy expansion.

insights.claim: Somalia's maritime chokepoints from the Gulf of Aden to the Suez Canal account for 12 to 15 percent of global trade and 30 percent of the world's container traffic, making its security a Red Sea trade issue rather than purely counterterrorism.
insights.speaker: Maureen Farrell
insights.so_what: Sizes why Somalia/Red Sea security matters to global shipping costs—relevant to freight rates and supply-chain rerouting risk.
insights.quote: the maritime chokepoints that Somalia sits astride...account for 12 to 15 percent of global trade and 30 percent of the world’s container traffic.
insights.locator: US engagement needs a clearer purpose

insights.claim: Iran-backed Houthi activity is disrupting Red Sea trade arteries, driving up shipping costs and forcing rerouting around South Africa.
insights.speaker: Maureen Farrell
insights.so_what: Ongoing Bab al-Mandeb disruption sustains elevated freight costs and longer voyage times—a structural shipping-cost tailwind/risk.

insights.claim: The authors argue Somalia may be one of the most overlooked medium/long-term investment opportunities in Africa, with underexplored offshore oil and gas, minerals, ports, fisheries, and logistics corridors—though they caution it remains fragile, violent, and politically fragmented.
insights.speaker: Maureen Farrell
insights.so_what: Flags a high-risk frontier resource play for patient capital tied to security—watch security/political stabilization as the unlock.
insights.quote: Somalia may be one of the most overlooked medium and long-term investment opportunities in Africa

insights.claim: Somaliland authorities have pursued international energy and minerals partnerships and highlighted potential battery-mineral supply-chain opportunities, though far more geological work is needed.
insights.speaker: Maureen Farrell
insights.so_what: An early, speculative battery-mineral angle in a contested territory—long-dated optionality contingent on recognition and geology.

insights.claim: Turkey has spent years building integrated influence in Somalia via military training, port/airport investment, maritime cooperation and now its first overseas deep-sea drilling mission off the Somali coast, showing an integrated security-diplomacy-commerce model.
insights.speaker: Maureen Farrell
insights.so_what: Turkey is the first mover linking security to energy in Somalia—a competitive template and signal of where commercial value is seen.
insights.quote: Its first overseas deep-sea drilling mission off the Somali coast shows how Ankara is trying to integrate security, diplomacy, and commercial ambition.

insights.claim: The authors urge the US to concentrate assistance where terrorist threats, cooperative local partners, and economic potential converge—naming Jubaland, Puntland, and Somaliland—rather than professionalizing Somalia's entire security sector.
insights.speaker: Maureen Farrell
insights.so_what: A geography-specific allocation rule signals where US-backed security and commercial activity would concentrate first.
insights.locator: Invest where security and economics converge

insights.claim: The Horn is becoming a crowded proxy arena—Turkey, UAE, Qatar, Egypt, Ethiopia, Saudi Arabia, Israel and Iran all have interests—and the US recently lifted sanctions on Eritrea, signaling a shift to treating the Horn's shoreline as a single maritime theatre.
insights.speaker: Maureen Farrell
insights.so_what: Intensifying multi-power competition raises both opportunity and instability risk in the Red Sea theatre—an evolving geopolitical map.
insights.locator: A strategy, not just a campaign
